perturbation.original_text: 13.2 Indemnification by Licensee. Subject to Section 13.4 (and notwithstanding any other indemnification obligation assumed by Licensee under this Agreement), Licensee agrees to indemnify and hold Bioeq harmless from and against all Claims for Losses to the extent arising out of or related to: (a) Licensee's breach of any representation, warranty, covenant or obligation under this Agreement; (b) Licensee's Commercialization of the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory; or (c) Licensee's negligence, recklessness, or wilful, intentional or criminal wrongdoing; except, in each case of (a)-(c) hereunder, to the extent such Losses are due to the events described in Section 13.1(a)-(b) above.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2 Indemnification by Licensee. Subject to Section 13.4 (and notwithstanding any other indemnification obligation assumed by Licensee under this Agreement), Licensee agrees to indemnify and hold Bioeq harmless from and against all Claims for Losses to the extent arising out of or related to: (a) Licensee's breach of any representation, warranty, covenant or obligation under this Agreement; (b) Licensee's actions related to the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory; or (c) Licensee's actions; except, in each case of (a)-(c) hereunder, to the extent such Losses are due to the events described in Section 13.1(a)-(b) above.
perturbation.explanation: By broadening the indemnification to include any 'actions' of the Licensee, and removing specific clauses regarding commercialization, negligence and wrongdoing, the clause becomes overly broad and could be interpreted to require Licensee to indemnify Bioeq even for issues arising from Bioeq's own actions or negligence related to the product. This may violate California's laws prohibiting exculpatory clauses in contracts and shifting liability for one's own negligence.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that 'All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt anyone from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law.' The modified clause could be interpreted to indemnify Bioeq even for their own negligence or willful misconduct, which is prohibited by this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the indemnification obligations of the Licensee to include 'actions related to the Licensed Products' and 'actions' in general, removing the specific clauses regarding commercialization, negligence, and wrongdoing. This creates a contradiction with California Civil Code Section 1668, as explained in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The snippet states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury, whether willful or negligent, are against public policy. By requiring the Licensee to indemnify Bioeq for potentially any 'actions,' including those arising from Bioeq's own negligence, the modified clause attempts to exempt Bioeq from responsibility for its own actions, which is explicitly prohibited by the law.

perturbation.original_text: 15.4 Non-Solicitation. Each Party agrees that, during the [***] ([***]) [***] period starting from the Effective Date, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment any employee of the other Party or its Affiliates or otherwise induce or attempt to induce such employees to terminate their employment with such other Party or such other Party's Affiliates; provided, however, that general public solicitations and advertisements not directed at employees of the other Party, and the extension of offers to persons who respond to such general solicitations and advertisements, will not be deemed violations of this provision. Upon breach of this non-solicitation obligation set forth in this Section 15.4, [***].
perturbation.changed_text: 15.4 Non-Solicitation. Each Party agrees that, during the [***] ([***]) [***] period starting from the Effective Date, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment any employee of the other Party or its Affiliates or otherwise induce or attempt to induce such employees to terminate their employment with such other Party or such other Party's Affiliates; provided, however, that general public solicitations and advertisements not directed at employees of the other Party, and the extension of offers to persons who respond to such general solicitations and advertisements, will not be deemed violations of this provision.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Upon breach of this non-solicitation obligation set forth in this Section 15.4, [***].', the contract omits any mention of remedy when this clause is violated. This impacts the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation clauses can be enforceable if narrowly tailored, omitting the consequences of breaching this provision weakens the agreement and could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if the lack of remedy makes the clause overly broad and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified consequences for breaching the non-solicitation agreement, ensuring enforceability. The changed text omits these consequences, which weakens the agreement and potentially renders it unenforceable. According to the Law Explanation, this omission could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' implying that non-solicitation agreements must be carefully constructed to avoid being overly restrictive. Removing the remedies for breach makes the restraint potentially unreasonable, thus contradicting the law.
